analysis.VISION:
Vision: "Our Vision is to commission a high-quality community centred health and care system by 2035." (p. 7, Section 1)
Mission: "Our mission is to commission healthcare services in Lancashire and South Cumbria to improve population health, reduce health inequalities and improve equitable access to consistently high-quality healthcare." (p. 7, Section 1)
analysis.10YP_ALIGNMENT:
The plan’s core is built on these shifts:
• Analogue to Digital: 
It features a dedicated Digital and Data Strategy (p. 46, Section 7) with priorities like a "Single Set of Core Strategic System Platforms" and "Unified Data Architecture" to enable this shift.
Page 21:
Making full use of the NHS App 
Using the NHS Federated Data Platform to improve care through the better use of data 
Deploying AI tools such as digital therapeutics and ambient voice 
• Hospital to Community: 
The plan’s primary vehicle is the development of "Neighbourhood Models" of care (p. 29, Section 4) to deliver care closer to home.
Page 21:
Accelerating progress on Neighbourhood Health 
Same day appointments for urgent cases in general practice 
Increasing community service capacity and productivity 
Greater use of community pharmacy 
Additional dental appointments 
• Sickness to Prevention: 
Prevention is a cross-cutting theme, with a dedicated "Prevention Framework" (p. 28, Section 4) and actions on "Tackling obesity... tobacco... vaccinations," directly responding to the 10 Year Plan's emphasis on upstream intervention.
Page 21:
Tackling obesity, including the continued roll out of weight loss medicines and weight management services 
Supporting the target of 25% reduction in CVD related premature mortality 
Implementing opt-out models of tobacco dependent services
Reducing antibiotic use and polypharmacy
analysis.10YP_DELIVERABILITY: The plan outlines a phased approach (p. 44, Section 6) with "Year 1: Grip and Stabilise (2026/27)" and "Year 2-3: Reset and Rebalance (2027-29)." The detailed commissioning intentions for 2026-27 (Section 8) translate the strategic shifts into concrete, immediate actions. For example, it includes intentions to "Recommission and mobilise Integrated Urgent Care services" (p. 58), "Establish a long-term commissioning approach... for tobacco dependency services" (p. 54), and "Drive forward universal adoption of digital citizen engagement tools" (p. 61). This shows a clear plan for early, practical steps that build towards the longer-term transformation.
analysis.TRADE-OFFS: addresses the need for resource reallocation. The plan acknowledges the "significant financial challenge" (p. 20) and states a need to move away from "historic funding baselines" (p. 43, Section 6). It commits to a deliberate "shift left" to progressively rebalance investment away from avoidable acute hospital activity towards neighbourhood and community-based models (p. 43). The plan also  uses a "Values Based Healthcare" framework (p. 13, Section 2) to guide investment choices, balancing personal, technical, allocative, and societal value, which inherently involves trade-offs to achieve long-term sustainability.
analysis.PREVENTION_READINESS:
The plan features a dedicated sub-section on "Prevention and Action on Health Inequalities" (p. 26, Section 4) and introduces a "Lancashire and South Cumbria Prevention Framework" (p. 28) covering primary, secondary, and tertiary prevention. Priorities include:
• Reducing tobacco dependency (p. 28, 54, 64).
• Tackling obesity through weight loss treatments and management services (p. 23, 54, 64).
• Increasing vaccination uptake (p. 28, 64).
• Early diagnosis of cancer and CVD (p. 26-27, 34).
• Reducing antibiotic use (p. 23, 64).
These actions are targeted at reducing risk factors and catching illness earlier, directly aligning with the "sickness to prevention" shift.
analysis.DIGITAL_READINESS:
The plan outlines a dedicated "Digital and Data Strategy (2024-2029)" (p. 46, Section 7) with four core priorities:
1. Secure Digital Infrastructure.
2. Single Set of Core Strategic System Platforms (e.g., Electronic Patient Records, Shared Care Record).
3. Unified Data Architecture (e.g., Federated Data Platform).
4. Integrated Digital and Data Service Delivery Model ('OneLSC').
Specific 2026-27 commissioning intentions include rolling out the Shared Care Record, implementing electronic prescribing, and adopting citizen engagement platforms (p. 61, Section 8), directly supporting the "analogue to digital" shift.
analysis.CARE_CLOSE_TO_HOME: This is the plan's primary vehicle for transformation. The plan makes "Neighbourhood Health Models" a central strategic priority (p. 29, Section 4). It commits to developing these as the way to deliver integrated, proactive, person-centred care. The six components of the neighbourhood model are outlined (p. 30), and the plan commits to strengthening support for specific cohorts like older people and those with long-term conditions within these neighbourhoods (p. 29-30). It also notes that Blackburn with Darwen and Morecambe Bay have been selected as national "Neighbourhood Health Implementer sites" (p. 29), indicating a readiness to lead this agenda.
analysis.WORKFORCE_READINESS:
The plan acknowledges the need to move from short-term to "strategic, multi-year workforce planning" (p. 48, Section 7). Key strategic changes include:
• Supporting the development of new roles and multidisciplinary teams within neighbourhood models (p. 48).
• Developing the ICB's own workforce as strategic commissioners, with a focus on skills in data, analytics, and system leadership, supported by an Organisational Development (OD) plan (p. 10, 49).
• A commitment to a "phased approach to Organisational Development" (p. 50) focusing on culture, values, and team effectiveness.
• Reducing reliance on premium-pay staffing through service redesign (p. 35).
These points show a clear understanding that new care models require a different workforce culture and capability.
analysis.EQUITY_READINESS:
The ICB has set three unifying goals to reduce inequalities (p. 26, Section 4), including reducing the gap in healthy life expectancy. The plan embeds equity through:
• Data and Insight: Using disaggregated data to understand inequalities related to deprivation, ethnicity, and inclusion groups (p. 27).
• Commissioning and Service Design: Systematically considering health inequalities in all commissioning decisions and targeting investment towards populations with the poorest outcomes (p. 27).
• Proportionate Universalism: Committing to an approach where investment is weighted according to relative need and deprivation (p. 42).
• Targeted Approaches: Commissioning outreach for specific groups, such as improving access for people with learning disabilities (p. 27).
analysis.INNOVATION_TRANSFORMATION:
The plan identifies innovation as a key enabler (p. 46, Section 7). Specific transformations include:
• Adopting new contracting mechanisms like Integrated Health Organisations (IHOs) and alliance models to foster collaboration and integration (p. 12).
• Using a "Values Based Healthcare" framework to guide investment and resource allocation (p. 13).
• Harnessing AI and new technology through its digital strategy, including Ambient Voice Technology to reduce admin burden (p. 47).
• Partnering with "Health Innovation Northwest" to facilitate the adoption and spread of innovation (p. 51).
These demonstrate a commitment to systemic innovation, not just technological fixes.
analysis.ADAPTABILITY:
It states it "is not a static document" and "will continue to be refined and iterated to reflect the changing environment" (p. 69, Section 10). This adaptability is built into its structure:
• It will be "refreshed annually" (p. 6, Section 1).
• It has a clear monitoring framework (p. 66-67, Section 9) to track delivery and impact, which would highlight emerging risks.
• The commissioning intentions are split into "Immediate priorities" for 2026-27 and "Medium Term Priorities" (Section 8), allowing for course correction based on learning from early implementation.

Our mission
To deliver safe, effective, sustainable care for everyone, everyday.

We will improve the lives of people who live, work and volunteer on the Fylde Coast and beyond.
analysis.DELIVERY_SHORT: No high-level plan provided, so presumed to be picked up in 3 year numerical plans.
analysis.10YP_ALIGNMENT: Section 5.8 (p.20) covers this directly.
analysis.10YP_DELIVERABILITY:
Section 1.5-1.6 (Page 3-4): The plan is candid about the immediate challenges to deliverability. It states it is "not yet compliant against the performance targets" and requires significant extra funding (£29m in 26/27) and workforce to bridge the gap. Its financial plan to reach balance by year 3 (28/29) is a critical enabler.
The plan includes specific productivity and efficiency initiatives (e.g., "optimised skill mix," "reduction of unwarranted variation") which are essential for delivering the 10 Year Plan's goals within financial constraints (p.21).
The transformation priorities a "Urgent and Emergency Care Programme" and "Waste Reduction Programme" are tangible delivery vehicles (p.25).
analysis.TRADE-OFFS:
The plan  states that delivering the financial control totals requires a significant trade-off in workforce, with a "c. 1,100 WTE headcount reduction over the first 3-year period" (p.4) as part of a £38.5m Waste Reduction Programme. 
It demonstrates a shift in financial resources by planning to move from a £19.9m deficit in 26/27 to a break-even position in 27/28, implying a reallocation of resources from deficit support to frontline or transformation funding (p.23).
Section 7.3: The plan discusses a system-level resource shift, aiming to "shift resources safely into community settings" through collaborative pathway redesign, directly addressing the "hospital to community" trade-off.
analysis.PREVENTION_READINESS:
2.1.4 (Page 5): The plan highlights the system's focus on "disease prevention and earlier intervention" as a core strategic shift, aligning with the 10 Year Plan.
3.4 (Page 10): Specific "demand management" actions are prevention-focused, such as:
•	Working with primary care providers to remove unwarranted variation in GP referrals.
•	Improving the utilisation of Care Coordination, virtual wards and frailty initiatives in order to reduce UEC demand.
5.8.1 (Page 20): The workforce strategy  supports the "Shift to Prevention and Population Health" through "expansion of public health, community, and primary care roles" and "development of health coaching and social prescribing capacity."
7.2 (Page 25): The "Our Population" pillar prioritises "prevention and early detection, especially for disadvantaged groups, reducing avoidable demand."
analysis.DIGITAL_READINESS:
4.1 (Page 11-15): This section provides a comprehensive table of digital delivery areas, directly addressing the "analogue to digital" shift. Priorities include:
- Patient Engagement Portal: To improve communication and reduce 'Did Not Attend' (DNA) rates.
- Ambient Voice Technology: To enhance clinical productivity (citing a 14% gain from a pilot).
- Federated Data Platform: To standardise validation and improve pathways.
- People Digital Programme: To automate joiners/movers/leavers processes.
1.9 (Page 4): Confirms that digital transformations are foundational, underpinning performance, workforce, and financial plans.
analysis.CARE_CLOSE_TO_HOME:
2.1.4, 2.1.8 (Page 5): The plan  acknowledges the shift to "moving care closer to home" and notes that at "Blackpool Place, partners are working together... to develop neighbourhood health models locally."
3.4 (Page 10): Actions to "improve the utilisation of Care Coordination, virtual wards and frailty initiatives" are designed to reduce UEC demand by providing care in community settings.
5.8.2 (Page 20): The workforce strategy details a "Shift to Community and Out-of-Hospital Care" by increasing community nursing capacity and developing "integrated neighbourhood teams."
7.3 (Page 25): The plan commits to "strengthening prevention and early intervention, improving access to community‑based alternatives to hospital care," enabling the 'left shift'.
analysis.WORKFORCE_READINESS:
5.7.2 (Page 19): The strategic workforce vision includes a pillar on "Flexible and Integrated Working," promoting "multidisciplinary teams" and "system-wide workforce planning," which are fundamental changes in ways of working.
5.8.2, 5.9.1 (Page 20-21): The plan outlines new workforce models, including "integrated neighbourhood teams" and "optimised skill mix and role redesign" (e.g., expansion of advanced practitioners), moving away from traditional siloed working.
5.3.1 (Page 17): It acknowledges cultural challenges, such as having "one of the highest sickness in the NHS," and outlines mitigations like "Sickness Clinics" and a "culture improvement plan" (2.4.7, Page 9).
5.10.3 (Page 22): A unique point is the recognition of the trust's role in influencing the local economy and considering "wider metrics such as local unemployment rates, literacy rates" as part of its workforce responsibility.
analysis.EQUITY_READINESS:
1.3, 2.2.6 (Page 3, 6): The plan repeatedly grounds itself in the context of serving "one of the most deprived populations in England" with the "lowest" male healthy life expectancy. This demonstrates that addressing inequality is a core driver, not an add-on.
2.1.8 (Page 5): It states that partners are "working together with a shared focus on reducing inequalities," showing system-level commitment.
5.2 (Page 16-17): The workforce section provides a detailed analysis of local deprivation, unemployment, and low skills levels, framing these as barriers to both health and employment that the trust, as a major employer, must address.
9.1 (Page 29): The plan has a mandatory "Equality and Quality Impact Assessment (EQIA/QIA)" process for all transformation programmes, ensuring equity is formally considered and risks are mitigated.
analysis.INNOVATION_TRANSFORMATION:
4.1 (Page 11-15): The digital roadmap is a core innovation strand, including system-wide projects like a single EPR, LIMS, and a federated data platform, which are transformative for care delivery and efficiency. "BTH is also currently in receipt of significant NHS frontline digitisation funds, it is one of only 6 trusts nationally not have an EPR. The anticipated rollout out of further frontline digitation e.g. Ambient Dictation (planned in 26/27) and the procurement and deployment of an EPR is expected to support material changes to both how the current and future workforce operates and is deployed but our short- and medium-term resource to enable this hugely complex delivery." (p.17)
7.1 (Page 24): The plan outlines a dual approach to transformation: "a mature Quality Improvement (QI) methodology and a delivery focused Programme Management Office (PMO)." This provides a structured, evidence-based engine for change.
7.2 (Page 24-25): Transformation priorities are clearly defined, including a "Waste Reduction Programme" using rigorous governance and a commitment to "GIRFT-aligned service reviews" to reduce unwarranted variation.
2.1.7 (Page 5): The agreement to merge pathology services across four trusts is a tangible example of system-level service transformation to improve efficiency.
analysis.ADAPTABILITY:
1.5, 6.6 (Page 3, 23): The plan demonstrates adaptability in its own creation. It  states that a contract offer received after the drafting period will "materially change" the submission in March, showing a responsive planning process to new funding realities.
3.1-3.3 (Page 10): The delivery approach is built on "close working relationships with System partners" to co-develop "demand mitigation schemes," indicating a flexible strategy that adapts to system-wide challenges and opportunities.
5.5.1, 5.5.4 (Page 18): The workforce plan acknowledges that "reconfiguration of services... will lead to different workforce models" and that "investment decisions support workforce development and sustainability," showing an adaptable approach to workforce planning based on service change.
Risks (Page 27-28): The comprehensive risk register, with identified mitigations for each major risk (e.g., no contract offer, WRP development, data quality), demonstrates a capacity to adapt plans in response to potential challenges.
DELIVERY: • Achieve break-even (financial balance) without deficit support.
• Implement new divisional structure (5 to 3 divisions).
• Develop sustainable talent pipelines aligned to local demographics.
• Begin c. 1,100 WTE headcount reduction (over 3 years).
• Embed new workforce models from service reconfiguration.
• Full implementation of new workforce models.
• Further improve performance against national targets.
• Continue progress towards full constitutional standards.
• Sustain performance against all constitutional standards.
• Introduce new SPOA models in two high-volume specialties.
• Embed demand management into core pathways.
• Deliver "outpatients as we know it" transformation.
• Improve RTT, 62-day cancer, diagnostic, and A&E 4-hour performance vs. Oct 2025.
• Implement single PACS/RIS solution with partners.
• Mature digital infrastructure (EPR, Federated Data Platform) driving productivity and population health management.
• Further integrate Federated Data Platform modules.
• Transfer pathology services to Lancashire Teaching Hospitals (1 April).
• Participate in service reconfiguration (e.g., vascular, orthodontics) as per system blueprint.
• Fully integrated system pathways, with resources shifted closer to home.
• Collaborate on demand growth reduction schemes with ICB.
• Explore One LSC shared corporate functions.
• Deliver Neighbourhood Health models at Blackpool Place.

analysis.10YP_ALIGNMENT:
p. 3, Executive Summary: The plan is  developed to "implement the ambitions of the 10 Year Health Plan."
p. 8, sec 2.2: It outlines how population health challenges (deprivation, inequitable access) directly shape the plan.
p. 38-40, sec 7.2: A dedicated table maps ELHT's plans directly to the ICB's vision, quadruple aims, strategic objectives, operating model, and the three 10YHP shifts (Sickness to Prevention, Care Closer to Home, Analogue to Digital). This shows a high degree of intentional alignment.
analysis.10YP_DELIVERABILITY:
p. 10-11, sec 3.1: The plan phases delivery into three periods: Year 1 (Stabilisation) focuses on foundational activities, eradicating corridor care, and exiting the Recovery Support Programme (RSP). Years 2-3 (Transforming) focuses on transforming care models and achieving a balanced exit run rate by March 2029.
p. 43, Risks: It acknowledges key delivery risks, such as the "affordability of delivering compliant plan" and "commissioning intentions for demand management not achieved," but assesses these with mitigations, suggesting a realistic view of deliverability challenges.
analysis.TRADE-OFFS:
p. 33, sec 6.2: The financial strategy  shows a shift in resources. It plans to transition from reliance on Deficit Support Funding (DSF) – assuming £36.8m in 26/27, reducing to £0.8m in 28/29 – to a balanced exit run rate, implying a shift from short-term support to recurrent financial sustainability.
p. 30, Workforce Table: The workforce plan shows a clear trade-off, with a significant increase in staff in Year 1 (due to One LSC transfer and activity demands), followed by net reductions in Years 2 and 3 as Waste Reduction Programme (WRP) schemes take effect.
analysis.PREVENTION_READINESS:
p. 3, Executive Summary: The plan's strategic priorities include "shifting to proactive, preventative and community-based care."
p. 7, sec 2.2: It  states the plan "prioritises health creation, prevention and early intervention" to reduce avoidable demand and improve long-term outcomes.
p. 10, sec 3.1: The principle "patients will only be in hospital when care cannot be provided in another setting" is a core preventative driver.
p. 37, sec 7.1: The "Urgent and Emergency Care Improvement" programme aims for upstream work on prevention, targeting "rising tide" users.
analysis.DIGITAL_READINESS:
p. 20-22, sec 4.2: A comprehensive table outlines key digital priorities, including: a single ICS-wide EPR, Patient Engagement Portal (PEP) , Ambient Voice Technology (AVT) , single PACS/RIS, People Digital Programme, and an ICS wide data lake.
p. 20, sec 4.2: The digital strategy aims to be "digital by default, where it is safe to do so," and to free up clinical time through tools like AVT and robotic process automation.
analysis.CARE_CLOSE_TO_HOME:
p. 3, Executive Summary: A key priority is to "work with primary care and our Place partners to provide care closer to home, through our neighbourhoods."
p. 5-6, sec 2.1: It provides evidence of success, citing the "Lancashire Model of Intermediate Care" and integrated neighbourhood teams (INTs) leading to decreased emergency admissions and re-admissions.
p. 36-37, sec 7.1: The plan details joint work at "Place" to fully embrace the "left shift," targeting specific wards with high health inequalities and designing local services around community hubs.
analysis.WORKFORCE_READINESS:
p. 27-28, sec 5.1: The plan prioritises putting "workforce first," being a "supportive, inclusive and compassionate organisation," and developing the "workforce for the future."
p. 28, sec 5.1: It outlines a multi-year workforce focus, from supporting wellbeing in Year 1, to empowering staff with improvement ideas in Years 2-3, to becoming a top-rated employer in Years 4-5.
p. 34, sec 7.1: It describes embedding its "Improvement Practice (SPE+)" at the heart of its culture, combining it with a Programme Management Office (PMO) for delivery.
p. 45, sec 10: It details a strengthened leadership and governance framework following external reviews, including a Board development programme.
analysis.EQUITY_READINESS:

p. 44, Risks: A specific "Health Equity" delivery risk is identified, with detailed mitigations including embedding health equity impact assessments, targeting neighbourhood-level interventions, and strengthening partnerships with VCFSE organisations.

p. 35-37, sec 7.1: The plan details system-level transformation through the Lancashire and South Cumbria Provider Collaborative (PCB) , working on clinical reconfiguration (e.g., vascular, urogynaecology) and corporate transformation (One LSC).
p. 9, sec 3.1: The long-term ambition is to form an Integrated Health Organisation (IHO) with Place partners.

analysis.VISION: "To provide the best mental health, learning disability, autism and community‑based services for the populations we serve."	p. 5
analysis.10YP_ALIGNMENT:
p. 4, Executive Summary:  states that the "Ten-Year NHS plan is the framework for becoming Fit for the Future" and that the Trust has "mapped the key requirements from the Ten-Year Health Plan against the current Trust Strategy," finding its key points already included.
p. 5: The Quadruple Aim (Best possible care, Joy and Pride in Work, Improving Health, Value for money) and 12 Strategic Goals provide a framework that reflects the 10YHP's ambitions.
analysis.10YP_DELIVERABILITY:
p. 4, Executive Summary: Identifies four specific NHS Oversight Framework (NOF) areas for "rapid improvement" with "detailed plans... in place," including sickness absence, crisis response, long-stay discharges, and urgent community response.
p. 11-12: Provides a trajectory forecasting improvement from NOF Segment 4 to Segment 3 by Q1 2026/27. It also sets out 3-year trajectories for key 10YHP mental health metrics, such as reducing Adult Acute Length of Stay from 61.5 days to 40 days by March 2029 (p. 12).
analysis.TRADE-OFFS:
p. 4, Executive Summary: Describes a "virtuous circle of increasing community investment and reducing reliance on inpatient care,"  linking resource shift to the "hospital to community" shift.
p. 21-22, Table 5: The Efficiency Improvement Programme (EIP) for 2026/27 totals £17.7m, with a risk-based assessment of schemes. It notes that where schemes imply staffing reductions, exit costs may be a "call on the Trust's ambitions," indicating a trade-off between savings and one-off costs.
p. 22, Table 6: Shows a planned £20.5m total reduction in bank and agency spend over three years, shifting resources from temporary to substantive staffing or other areas.
analysis.PREVENTION_READINESS:
p. 7-8: The health inequalities plan embeds "prevention" as a core principle, using population health intelligence to enable "proactive, preventative approaches" and targeting upstream interventions.
p. 12: The plan to reduce Adult Acute Length of Stay is driven by a "left-shift investment in community services, particularly Assertive Engagement functions... and through strengthened neighbourhood and Place partnerships," enabling earlier intervention and preventing admissions.
analysis.DIGITAL_READINESS:
p. 14-15, Table: Provides a detailed adoption plan for core digital products, including integrating the Patient Engagement Portal with the NHS App by Q1 26/27, adhering to the Federated Data Platform (FDP) policy, and using Electronic Patient Records (EPRs).
p. 16, 29: The workforce plan highlights "Analogue to Digital" as a key theme, with plans for ambient voice technology and digital dictation in 2026/27. The Digital enabler plan (p. 29) outlines using AI (Microsoft Copilot, ambient dictation) to release time to care.
analysis.CARE_CLOSE_TO_HOME:
p. 4, 17, 25: The Executive Summary (p. 4) mentions "investment into community teams." The workforce plan (p. 17) notes work is "underway to explore what that transformation looks like for our workforce through the establishment of neighbourhood hubs." The transformation section (p. 25) states the Trust is "strengthening neighbourhood working as a core mechanism for delivering integrated, place‑based models of care."
p. 30-31: The Estates enabler plan details plans to develop "Community Roots' neighbourhood mental health centres" and "24/7 Neighbourhood MH Centres (NMHCs)" to deliver care on a neighbourhood footprint.
analysis.WORKFORCE_READINESS:
p. 5, 16: The Quadruple Aim includes "Joy and Pride in Work." The Workforce Strategic Vision (p. 16) states colleagues "are our most valuable asset" and aims for them to "feel valued, experience joy and feel pride."
p. 16: A detailed "colleague attendance improvement plan" addresses high sickness rates through prevention, leadership, evidence-based approaches, digital tools, and grip and control.
p. 17: Describes a collaborative approach to workforce planning involving transformation, professional leads, recruitment, PMO, and clinical networks.
analysis.EQUITY_READINESS: p. 7-8: Provides a comprehensive overview of its approach, stating it is "embedding an equity lens into every core element of planning, transformation and operational delivery." Key actions include using population health intelligence, integrating health inequality objectives into appraisals, co-design with communities via VCFSE partnerships, and addressing wider determinants (e.g., homelessness, employment). This directly addresses the principles of CORE20PLUS5.
analysis.INNOVATION_TRANSFORMATION:
p. 9-10, 25-26: The Trust's improvement methodology, LSCI (Lancashire and South Cumbria improvement approach) , is its core innovation framework for embedding continuous improvement. Transformation is delivered through an "integrated, system‑facing model" combining pathway redesign, value management, and neighbourhood working (p. 25).

analysis.VISION:
Vision
"We want everyone in Cheshire and Merseyside to have a great start in life and get the support they need to stay healthy and live healthier for longer." (p. 5, About Cheshire and Merseyside)
Mission
"We will prevent ill health and tackle health inequalities and improve the lives of the poorest the fastest. We believe we can do this best by working in partnership." (p. 5, About Cheshire and Merseyside)
analysis.10YP_ALIGNMENT:
Page 18 is a dedicated section on supporting 10 year shifts.
The plan's introduction also states it is "Aligned with the NHS 10-Year Health Plan" and focuses on "transforming services to improve outcomes" by delivering the "three key shifts in care: Hospital to community, Sickness to prevention, Analogue to digital" (p. 4, Introduction). A dedicated diagram on page 18 illustrates how the plan's priorities support these three shifts, and the "All Together Fairer" plan (p. 12) reinforces the prevention agenda.
analysis.10YP_DELIVERABILITY: The plan outlines a clear five-year transition to new models, e.g., a "five-year transition from activity-based contracts to outcomes-focused approaches" (p. 7, How will we work differently). It identifies specific early actions, such as a review of maternity and neonatal services (p. 21) and rolling out a Local Enhanced Service for adult ADHD (p. 24). However, unlike some other plans, it does not provide a consolidated table of year-by-year targets for core metrics (e.g., RTT, UEC) against which to judge first-three-year delivery. The focus is on setting the strategic direction and building the foundations for change.
analysis.TRADE-OFFS: The plan acknowledges the system is "spending above its fair-share allocation" with an "underlying financial deficit of over £400m" (p. 14, 32). It commits to shifting resources "toward prevention and proactive care through a transformation fund" (p. 32) and to "decommission low-value interventions" (p. 15, The principles). The goal is to redirect investment from "costly hospital-based care" to community-based alternatives, driven by a needs assessment that shows the frailty population alone is projected to drive a £297m cost increase (p. 14).
analysis.PREVENTION_READINESS:
Prevention is embedded through the "Living Healthy Lives" section (p. 20), which links to the "All Together Fairer" plan based on the Marmot review (p. 12). Specific prevention priorities include: 
-Wider Determinants: Action on work, housing, and fuel poverty via the Health and Care Partnership (p. 12).
-Risk Factors: Targeted work on smoking, unhealthy weight, and vaccination uptake, particularly in deprived communities (p. 20).
-Life Course Approach: Priorities for children (oral health, obesity), mental health, and frailty all have strong prevention elements (p. 22-25).
Specific focus on "PLUS" groups (p.8)
analysis.DIGITAL_READINESS:
The plan aims to create a "shared multi-year digital workflow" through a "Digital Centre of Excellence" to coordinate innovation and drive transformation (p. 29). Key priorities are:
1. Secure and share patient information via a single record across settings.
2. Enable digital clinical services to improve workflows and empower patients.
3. Harness data for insight using platforms like CIPHA and the Federated Data Platform (p. 15).
4. Embrace bold innovation, including AI, to radically transform operations (p. 29). The 5-year roadmap is anchored to goals of levelling up digital maturity and harmonising systems (p. 29).
analysis.CARE_CLOSE_TO_HOME:
A dedicated section on "Neighbourhood Health" (p. 19) describes it as the model to deliver proactive, preventative care through integrated teams. Key elements include:
• Integrated Neighbourhood Teams bringing together NHS, local government, VCFSE, housing, and education.
• Delegated whole-population budgets to neighbourhood partnerships (p. 19).
• Specific actions: Improving the primary-secondary care interface, expanding community diagnostics, and supporting people with complex needs (p. 19). The model is to be implemented locally by nine Health and Wellbeing Boards (p. 19).
analysis.WORKFORCE_READINESS:
The plan references a "System Workforce Plan" that frames the workforce as key to enabling the shift to community-based care (p. 30). Priorities include:
-Pursuing affordable workforce models aligned with new integrated neighbourhood models.
-Rationalising workforce resources and transforming roles to support prevention and community care (p. 30).
-Cultural change: A strong emphasis on creating anti-racist, psychologically safe workplaces, delivering on the NHS People Promise, and embedding staff voice (p. 30).
analysis.EQUITY_READINESS:
The vision is to "improve the lives of the poorest fastest" (p. 5, 17). The plan is grounded in the "All Together Fairer" agenda (p. 12), which applies Marmot's principles. Specific actions include:
-CORE20PLUS5: The "PLUS groups" are  mentioned as a focus for using community insight to remove barriers (p. 8).
-Targeted Priorities: Frailty actions note that a third of falls admissions are from the most deprived communities (p. 25). End of life care aims to tackle inequalities where deprived communities are less likely to die at home (p. 26). Maternity priorities focus on higher risks for women from deprived and ethnic minority backgrounds (p. 21).
analysis.INNOVATION_TRANSFORMATION:
Key transformations include:
• New Contract Models: Plans for Integrated Health Organisations, accountable care approaches (e.g., for children and mental health), lead provider models, and neighbourhood-based contracts (p. 7, 9).
• Digital Innovation: Establishing a Digital Centre of Excellence to harness AI and attract external investment (p. 29).
• Service Reconfiguration: Formal programmes to consolidate fragile services, e.g., a review of maternity and neonatal services (p. 21) and developing provider collaboratives (p. 8).
• Population Health Management: Using risk stratification tools (CIPHA, Federated Data Platform) to target interventions (p. 15, 20).
analysis.ADAPTABILITY:
It  states it is "at the beginning of this journey" and will need to "rapidly strengthen our capability" (p. 6). Adaptability is built in through:
• Phased capability building: A three-stage approach to developing strategic commissioning capability: "Setting the foundations" (2026/27), "Building capability," and "Transformation" (p. 10).
• Iterative planning: The plan is based on an "updated integrated needs assessment" (p. 6) and commits to using continuous evaluation to "refine interventions" (p. 9).
• Flexible geography: It commits to commissioning at the "most appropriate footprint" – regional, system, place, or neighbourhood – depending on the service and population need (p. 7).

analysis.SUMMARY: Mid Cheshire; priority cohorts from patient-level segmentation. | Models of Care directly translate three shifts locally. | Front-loaded recovery creates headroom; success depends on delivery capacity and system flow. | 6% Year 1 CIP and 707 WTE reduction to stabilise services. | Help Me Stay Well model targets frailty and long-term conditions. | Phased adoption to stabilise, enable redesign, then advance. | Phased neighbourhood scaling based on evidence, not uniform rollout. | Supporting people through necessary redesign, not just headcount reduction. | Equity risks from sequencing explicitly assessed and mitigated. | Healthier Futures programme; Alliance and Christie partnerships. | Living plan with explicit refresh triggers and re-phasing.
analysis.VISION:
"A healthier future for our communities by delivering high-quality, connected care through neighbourhoods and Place-based working." p. 7

“We provide high-quality, connected care across hospital, community and neighbourhood settings — creating a healthier future for the people of Cheshire through prevention, personalisation and partnership.”
This vision is grounded in a life-course and neighbourhood approach, focusing on earlier intervention, better continuity and local support, alongside safe and productive hospital services. (p.20)
analysis.10YP_ALIGNMENT: Plan is built around the three shifts: hospital to community, sickness to prevention, analogue to digital (p. 3, 6-7). Aligned to Cheshire and Merseyside ICB's strategic commissioning plan and Joint Forward Plan (p. 118-119). The Trust's five life-course Models of Care directly translate national ambition into local delivery logic (p. 26-27).
analysis.10YP_DELIVERABILITY:
The plan deliberately front-loads financial and workforce recovery to create headroom for transformation (p. 8, 10): "The Board has taken a deliberate decision to pursue a higher-risk delivery approach in the early years of the IDTP... This approach reflects a conscious acceptance of elevated delivery, workforce and regulatory risk in the short term, rather than deferring risk or embedding longer-term structural fragility."

Referral to Treatment (RTT) 92% and cancer standards are delivered by Year 3 (p. 112). Urgent and Emergency Care (UEC) is stabilised at 78% by Year 1, with improvement towards 85% set as direction of travel beyond Year 3 (p. 10, 112). Neighbourhood health is scaled selectively in Years 2-3, with full maturity beyond the plan (p. 60). The shift is therefore achieved in stages, with delivery confidence explicitly moderated.
analysis.TRADE-OFFS: A front-loaded Cost Improvement Programme (CIP) of 6% in Year 1 (p. 52) and a workforce reduction of c. 707 Whole Time Equivalent (WTE) over three years (p. 10, 43) are accepted to create financial headroom and stabilise services. This represents a deliberate decision to accept higher short-term risk to avoid embedding longer-term structural fragility (p. 8). Up to £3m of targeted investment is available for critical quality and safety risks, but is tightly governed (p. 52).
analysis.PREVENTION_READINESS:
"Sickness to prevention" is a core shift (p. 3). Priorities include: neighbourhood-based multidisciplinary care, anticipatory support for frail and complex patients, virtual wards, hospital-at-home, and integrated care for people with multiple long-term conditions (p. 22-23, 31). The "Help Me Stay Well" Model of Care is explicitly prevention-focused (p. 26-27).

Priorities:
•Neighbourhood-based multidisciplinary care and anticipatory support, particularly for people living with frailty, long-term conditions and complex needs, delivered in partnership with primary care, local authorities and the voluntary sector.
•Virtual wards and hospital-at-home models, selectively scaled where evidence demonstrates impact on admissions, length of stay, patient experience and safety.
•Same Day Emergency Care and ambulatory pathways, reducing avoidable admissions and improving urgent and emergency care flow.
•Redesigned elective pathways, including pre-operative optimisation, straight-to-test diagnostics and one-stop models, supporting elective recovery and productivity.
•Improved diagnostic pathways and turnaround, as a critical enabler of elective, cancer and urgent care performance.
•Cancer pathway redesign, including faster diagnostics and improved pathway coordination, to support recovery against national standards.
•Reliable discharge and transfer of care models, including criteria-led discharge and strengthened interfaces with community and neighbourhood services.
•Digitally enabled care coordination, using core EPR functionality to support safer clinical decision-making, flow and productivity.
analysis.DIGITAL_READINESS: Digital enablement is a key enabler (p. 56-58). Priorities include: real-time visibility of patient status and flow, digitally supported diagnostics ordering and results management, electronic documentation, pathway-level performance insight, and adoption of the Federated Data Platform (p. 57-58). Digital is sequenced to support stabilisation (Year 1), scaling (Years 2-3), and advanced use (Years 4-5) (p. 57-58).
analysis.CARE_CLOSE_TO_HOME: "Hospital to community" is a core shift (p. 3). The Trust's role as a neighbourhood integrator is defined (p. 30). Priorities include: virtual wards, hospital-at-home, integrated neighbourhood teams, improved intermediate care and discharge pathways, and community-based diagnostics (Crewe Diagnostic Hub) (p. 31-32, 60). Delivery is phased, with selective scaling in Years 2-3 based on evidence and partner readiness (p. 60).
analysis.WORKFORCE_READINESS:
Workforce is treated as the primary constraint (p. 17, 43). The plan prioritises supporting colleagues through change, with a focus on retention, wellbeing, and safety (p. 45). Leadership, engagement, and capability are strengthened through the "Thriving Culture" programme, values, and leadership development (p. 48-49). Productivity is framed as an outcome of redesign, not intensification (p. 48).
Productivity improvement within the IDTP is expected to arise primarily from deliberate service redesign, improved flow and digitally enabled ways of working. It is not assumed to come from incremental efficiency savings or from asking staff to work harder within existing models of care. (p.48).

Leadership and culture: Over the IDTP period, the Trust will continue to strengthen:
•Visible clinical and managerial leadership at all levels, aligned to the national Management & Leadership Framework.
•Inclusive and compassionate leadership behaviours, supporting psychological safety, belonging and engagement.
•Leadership capability for digital, neighbourhood-based and cross-boundary working.
•Meaningful engagement with colleagues and trade unions, particularly where roles, rotas or ways of working change.
•Practical improvement capability and support for teams leading change, especially in high-pressure services.
Together, these actions underpin retention,
analysis.EQUITY_READINESS:

Prioritised cohorts (frailty, multiple long-term conditions, children with additional needs) align with groups facing inequity (p. 19). Mitigations include targeted cohort focus, assisted access, and monitoring of variation (p. 125-126). Core20PLUS5 is referenced in the context of children and young people (p. 12).

analysis.INNOVATION_TRANSFORMATION: The "Healthier Futures" transformation programme is the primary vehicle for innovation (p. 38-42). analysis.ADAPTABILITY: The plan is explicitly designed as a "living medium-term plan" with formal refresh points (p. 13, 28). Governance through the Executive Integrated Transformation Group (EITG) and Board provides oversight and course correction (p. 74, 80-81).

Equity implications of sequencing are assessed in Appendix J, with mitigations for groups at risk of delayed benefit (p. 122-127).

analysis.SUMMARY: The plan is built on a detailed understanding of its specific geography. | Strong and explicit alignment to the 10 year plan shifts. | A plan is presented for core priorities e.g. elective care reform, community services transformation, with dates for other areas written in the narrative. | The plan  acknowledges the need for resource shift and outlines a method to achieve it. | Prevention is the foundational priorit | Digital is a core system enabler with a clear partnership approach. | Primary delivery vehicle for the plan's ambitions. | Workforce is addressed as a key component of system transformation, though less central than prevention or digital. | Equity is a core driver, deeply embedded in the plan's analysis and delivery. | Innovation is a core strategic theme, with a formal partnership to drive it. | The plan is  designed as a flexible starting point.
analysis.VISION:
Vision
System Vision: "A Thriving City Region Where Everyone Can Live a Good Life." (p. 5, Section 1.1). This is the vision for the overarching Greater Manchester Strategy, which the NHS GM plan contributes to.
Clinical Strategy Vision: "To create a healthier and fairer Greater Manchester by delivering high quality, integrated, person-centred care that tackles inequalities and empowers communities; supported through a culture of prevention and improvement, alongside clinical priorities to improve the access to and experience of health care as well as the health outcomes of our population" (p. 8, Section 1.2)
analysis.DELIVERY_SHORT:
Community Services Transformation (p. 26-27): This section provides a clear "Draft Priority Delivery Programmes / Actions (2026-2031)" broken down by:
Year 1 - Establish the Foundations: (e.g., GM-wide baselining, District Nursing Core Standards implementation).
Year 2 - Early Scaling: (e.g., GM Falls Prevention roll-out, expansion of community diagnostics).
Year 3 - Integrated Neighbourhood Model: (e.g., Live Well hubs operating, full GM Care Record utilisation).
Year 4 - Workforce Maturity and Productivity: (e.g., ACP and blended role expansion).
Year 5 - Optimisation and Evaluation: (e.g., whole-system evaluation, refresh strategy).
analysis.10YP_ALIGNMENT:
The plan is directly structured around the "three shifts" from the 10 Year Health Plan: treatment to prevention, hospital to community, and analogue to digital (Section 3.1). It also  references the 10 Year Health Plan as a key strategic document guiding its development (Section 1.1). Greater Manchester's designation as the national "Prevention Demonstrator" (p.9, Section 1.3) further cements this alignment, creating a direct feedback loop between local delivery and national policy.
Strategic themes cover each shift:
section 4.1 - prevention and shift to neighbourhood s
section 4.3 - system transformation (incl. digital and innovation)
Additionally, there is extra emphasis on how the 10 year plan shifts work in Greater Manchester (p.16):
1) The shift in system conditions and culture towards prevention demonstrated by what we measure and how we commission
2) The shift in the model of care from treatment to prevention
3) The shift in the location of care from acute settings to community and neighbourhood settings – including through digital models
analysis.10YP_DELIVERABILITY: The plan sets clear, measurable performance trajectories for core targets (RTT, UEC, Cancer) from March 2026 to March 2029 (p. 17, Section 3.2). Crucially, it breaks down delivery for key programmes into year-by-year actions. For example, the Elective Care Reform section provides a detailed table of commissioning priorities and expected outcomes for 2026/27, 2027/28, and 2028/29 (p. 33, Section 4.3). Similarly, the Community Services Transformation section outlines a Year 1-5 plan, with Years 1-3 focusing on establishing foundations, early scaling, and implementing the integrated neighbourhood model (p. 26).
analysis.TRADE-OFFS: It references the Darzi report's finding of a "right drift" (more spending on hospitals) and commits to a "left shift" (Section 3.1). A key part of the plan is establishing an "agreed system method for the modelling of the impact of the Left Shift" during 2026/27 to quantify impact and adjust provider contracts (Section 3.3). It also aims to increase the "proportion of overall spend on prevention" by deploying new contracting and financial mechanisms that incentivise it (p. 18). The goal to shift investment from acute to community is a central trade-off.
analysis.PREVENTION_READINESS:
As the "Prevention Demonstrator," this is the plan's most developed theme. It is operationalised through the 'Live Well' approach, a whole-system strategy to address the social determinants of health (Section 4.1). Priorities include:
• Targeted Action on Population Health: Detailed programmes on tobacco, alcohol, physical activity, and early detection of long-term conditions (p. 27).
• Wider Determinants: Explicit action on work, health, housing, and poverty (p. 28).
• Preventing Communicable Diseases: Programmes for HIV, TB, and BBV opt-out testing (p. 29).
• Children and Young People: A dedicated section on improving outcomes from the early years onwards (p. 29).
analysis.DIGITAL_READINESS:
The plan outlines a joint delivery programme with Health Innovation Manchester (HInM) (Section 4.3). Key priorities are on p.35:
-Optimising core infrastructure: Evolving the GM Care Record, Advanced Data Science Platform, and Secure Data Environment.
-Embedding AI-enabled transformation: Deploying AI to improve productivity and pathways.
-Using data for population health missions: Aligning analytics with priorities like cardiovascular disease.
-Becoming a "globally competitive learning system" for innovation and real-world evidence.
analysis.CARE_CLOSE_TO_HOME:
The Neighbourhood Model is central, described as the focal point where different elements of 'Live Well' come together (Section 4.1). Key features include:
• Multi-agency teams operating on footprints of 30-50k population, including health, council, DWP, police, and VCFSE (p. 20-21).
• Neighbourhood Health Plans being developed by all 10 localities, incorporating six national foundational steps (p. 21-22).
• A dedicated Community Services Transformation programme with a 5-year plan to shift diagnostics and care into the community and reduce avoidable admissions (p. 24).
analysis.WORKFORCE_READINESS:
he plan acknowledges the need for a "new workforce model with staff genuinely aligned with the future direction of reform" (p. 17, echoing 10YP). Specific mentions include:
• New Roles and Skills: In community services, the plan calls for "Growth in ACPs, blended ASC–NHS roles and proactive-care capability" and expects providers to adhere to job planning standards (p. 26-27).
• Digital and AI: The digital strategy aims to "alleviate workforce pressures" through AI and automation (p. 36).
• Strategic Commissioning Capability: The ICB will develop its own workforce skills in areas like market management and outcomes-based commissioning (p. 37-38).
analysis.EQUITY_READINESS:
The plan uses detailed IMD data to map inequalities (p. 12, Section 2.2) and identifies specific populations experiencing "lives (and deaths) of despair" due to multiple deprivation (p. 13). Actions include:
• CORE20PLUS5:  referenced for children and young people (p. 31) and as a focus for narrowing gaps via population health management (p. 26).
• Targeted Action: Programmes on infant mortality, school readiness, and specific condition pathways (p. 29).
• Embedded in Commissioning: A commitment to embed "mechanisms to reduce inequalities firmly within the strategic commissioning cycle" and ensure "a clear stance on anti-racism is built into all commissioning decisions" (p. 38).
analysis.INNOVATION_TRANSFORMATION:
The partnership with Health Innovation Manchester (HInM) is the primary vehicle, with a joint mission to "accelerate population health impact, improve system productivity and position Greater Manchester as a globally recognised learning system" (p. 35-36). Key innovations include:
• New Commissioning Models: Exploring outcomes-based contracts, value-based payments, pooled budgets, and Integrated Health Organisations (IHOs) (p. 37-38).
• AI and Data Science: Deploying AI for pathway optimisation and using the Secure Data Environment for real-world evidence (p. 36).
• The VCFSE Accord: A formal agreement with the voluntary sector to ensure financial fairness and shared accountability (p. 39).
analysis.ADAPTABILITY:
It states clearly that it "represents a starting point" and that there is "significant work to do on confirming individual programmes and quantifying the impact of delivery" (p. 4, Section 1.1). Adaptability is built in through:
• Annual Refinement: The plan will be supported by an "agreed system method for the modelling of the impact of the Left Shift" to be developed in 2026/27, which will inform future contract adjustments (p. 18).
• Monitoring and Evaluation: The Outcomes Framework (p. 8) and the real-time evaluation from the Prevention Demonstrator (p. 10) will provide data to "course correct if needed."
• Phased Delivery: Programmes like Elective Reform and Community Services are set out in clear phases, allowing for learning from early years to inform later stages (p. 27, 34).

analysis.10YP_DELIVERABILITY: Phased trajectory delivers 92% Referral to Treatment (RTT), 85% Accident & Emergency (A&E), 1% diagnostics, and cancer standards by 2028/29 (p. 10). analysis.VISION: MISSION: "Saving Lives, Improving Lives" – to be one of the safest, most effective and financially sustainable organisations in the NHS and the place where people want to work. (p.10)
analysis.10YP_ALIGNMENT: Aligned to the three shifts: analogue to digital, hospital to community, treatment to prevention (p. 35). analysis.VISION:
Extensive new corporate governance arrangements will be in place from 1 April 2026 that will see a new Joint Board come together alongside the development during 2026/27 of a new joint Organisational Strategy and enabling strategies that include a joint Quality Strategy and a Clinical Strategy.
Our formal collaborative arrangements are focussed on a shared vision for integrated care, designed to create a more resilient, innovative, and patient-centred healthcare system for both populations. (p.1)
analysis.10YP_ALIGNMENT:
Plan responds to 10 Year Health Plan priorities, aiming for a "significant shift" to seamless care delivered closer to home, and a supportive environment for staff (p. 1). Joint working arrangements designed to create a more resilient, innovative, patient-centred system (p. 1).
−From hospital to community: Emphasis on neighbourhood and preventative care models
−From analogue to digital: Increased use of AI, automation, and digital tools to free up clinical time, with consideration of the Trust Robotic Process Automation (RPA) opportunities.
−From sickness to prevention: Workforce roles will evolve to support proactive, personalised care (p.7)
analysis.10YP_DELIVERABILITY: Financial breakeven by 2030/31, not within first three years (p.4). Urgent and Emergency Care (UEC) performance remains non-compliant for both Trusts due to bed deficits and discharge challenges (p. 2-3). Delivery of elective targets contingent on additional ICB income (p. 2-3). Digital and estate enablers are foundational but require significant capital (p. 4, 6).
analysis.TRADE-OFFS: Both Trusts rely on Deficit Support Funding (DSF) and have been set a 5-year trajectory to breakeven (p. 2, 4). Cost Improvement Programme (CIP) at 5% per year, with a large proportion unidentified for 2026/27 (p. 5). Delivery of targets assumes additional ICB income and block contract deconstruction funding (p. 2-3, 6). Major service reconfiguration required over 3-5 years (p. 6).
analysis.PREVENTION_READINESS: Workforce plan references shift "from sickness to prevention" with roles evolving to support proactive, personalised care (p. 7). No specific prevention programmes detailed.
analysis.DIGITAL_READINESS: Digital priorities include: joint Electronic Patient Record (EPR), expanded patient-facing digital pathways, enhanced interoperability across acute, community, and primary care, upgraded core infrastructure, and use of analytics for population-level planning (p. 4). Robotic Process Automation (RPA) opportunities to be considered (p. 7).
analysis.CARE_CLOSE_TO_HOME: Vision includes "seamless care, delivered closer to home" (p. 1). Workforce plan references shift "from hospital to community" with emphasis on neighbourhood and preventative care models (p. 7). Stockport has 8 neighbourhoods; Tameside has 4 (p. 1). Single Point of Access (SPOA) and care home conveyance project aim to reduce unnecessary conveyances (p. 3).
analysis.WORKFORCE_READINESS: Joint People Strategy being refreshed (p. 6). Workforce planning uses five-year retrospective analysis to forecast trends (p. 6-7). Plan addresses three shifts: hospital to community, analogue to digital, sickness to prevention (p. 7). Focus on multidisciplinary teams, digital skills, apprenticeships, and retention (p. 7).
analysis.EQUITY_READINESS: Plan acknowledges "significant health inequalities" in both populations (p. 1). No explicit reference to Core20PLUS5 framework or specific equity interventions.
analysis.INNOVATION_TRANSFORMATION: "ADOPT" Continuous Improvement Strategic Model with 5 ambitions: Align, Develop, Organisational Partnerships, People at heart, Train (p. 8-9). Transformation priorities include: Emergency Department navigation, Outpatients (Advice & Guidance, Patient Initiated Follow-Up (PIFU)), Cancer Faster Diagnosis Standard, Single Point of Access (SPOA), Inpatient flow (p. 9).
analysis.ADAPTABILITY: Board Assurance Frameworks (BAF) for both Trusts track strategic risks (financial sustainability, operational delivery, quality of care, workforce, estates and digital) (p. 9-10). Joint Executive Oversight Group for planning (p. 2). Monthly cycle of meetings to monitor key metrics (p. 11). Plan acknowledges material financial risk and need for system support (p. 6).

analysis.VISION:

analysis.10YP_ALIGNMENT: Plan  aligned to three shifts: treatment to prevention, hospital to community, analogue to digital (p. 4-5).
